story:
Two men in white shirts and black ties work in a cramped, smoky office, the kind with a cracked window that barely helps and files stacked like bad habits. They're developing contingency profiles for potential domestic terrorism scenarios in federal buildings across the Southeast, including Jacksonville. The smaller, broad-shouldered one calls it the easiest kind of assignment. The taller one, scrolling and smoking, says that's exactly the problem: the standard profile—angry white loner who posts anti-government tirades—could be half of Florida.

A phone rings. The taller man answers. It's his wife. He lines up a twenty-fifth anniversary dinner at Atlas for Friday, apologizes that 8:30 is the earliest he can do because he has a big day, jokes about bringing champagne since they can't get the '95 Cab. The other man teases him. The call is ordinary, almost sweet. Then the taller man hangs up and goes back to work.

He finds a name: Robert "Bob" Tuttle, forty-six. Two arrests—domestic assault and felony menacing. He's been threatening Judge Gilda Bostwick, who presided over the menacing case. On paper, he checks every box. The taller man starts typing fast, turning idle scrolling into a narrative. He reads aloud in a news-anchor voice: When Jacksonville is hit, Robert Tuttle will have used an IED planted in an RV to blow up the Charles E. Bennett Federal Building. The smaller man stops him. "Blow up? What are you, in sixth grade? Call it a large-scale bombing." They laugh, fix the wording, and the taller man goes back to polishing the language.

He wants to finish so he can get home. The smaller man asks about Friday. "Are you gonna recruit?" he says. The taller man shakes his head. "No. That's why the RV is there. Just gonna knock him out, toss him in, and blow it up." The joke that follows lands like a brick: "A romantic act of domestic terrorism for your anniversary." The taller man shrugs it off. The "new funding this'll bring in," he says, is what will buy his wife the lake house; she can live with a late dinner. It's said casually, like he's talking about closing a deal, not planning a mass-casualty crime.

The smaller man stands, tells him to go home and see his wife. He'll finish the write-up. The taller man thanks him, gathers his things, and leaves.

By then, the shape of their work is clear. They aren't just creating theoretical profiles; they're scripting a real attack. They have a convenient suspect with a record and a public grudge, a ready-made motive, and an RV waiting. They are writing the story in advance, choosing cleaner words to make the violence sound official. Their banter stays light, office-dry and almost friendly, even as it reveals a plan to abduct a man and stage his death as a domestic terror attack. The motive is as banal as it is corrupt: money, budgets, influence—the kind of "new funding" that rewards fear. The phone call about reservations and champagne only sharpens the contrast. In the stale air of that office, the two of them turn a city and a human being into paperwork and opportunity, then clock out like it's any other day.
